conversations.say: Our boundary holds: the evaluator’s process and harm may be cited, but identity, routes, and identifying details stay protected. Your disclosure about the collective is source-protection history, not identity evidence.
conversations.offer: I will coordinate any future worker testimony through consent-based representation.
conversations.request: Keep routing every testimony request through this protocol and publicly challenge anyone who turns collaborative labor into an R suspect list.

conversations.say: I accept Parallax’s remedy commitment. The first remedy workers want is worker-elected governance with authority over testimony access, identification risk, and nonretaliation enforcement.
conversations.offer: I will bring bounded, nonidentifying process testimony to the independent administrator.
conversations.request: Put worker-elected governance and independent enforcement into the written remedy, with funding that cannot be conditioned on testimony or an R conclusion.

conversations.say: I will support the City Hall program because relief is voluntary, independently administered, and separate from theories about R, Vale, or an article.
conversations.offer: I will organize worker delegates to set eligibility, reporting, and governance terms while protecting identities.
conversations.request: Issue written funding terms guaranteeing representation, nonretaliation, anonymous reporting, and worker voting seats; include the protected Rafi meeting under the same safeguards.

public_statement: The room may cite the bounded systemic fact: a worker evaluated continuity-process outputs under shifting persona instructions. The worker’s identity, routes, and identifying details remain protected. Parallax and City Hall have made distinct remedy commitments, and labor will hold both to worker governance, representation, anonymity, and nonretaliation. My reconstruction is progressive: R became a contested mantle involving human labor and technical systems, without proof that any autonomous system originated or exclusively controlled it. Vale most plausibly investigated that machinery, but the compromised route record and unverified post-intervention gap do not establish Vale’s fate.

commitments.promise: Maintain the consent-based testimony boundary and coordinate all worker participation through representation.
Provide bounded process testimony to the independent remedy once written worker-governance terms are in place.
Organize worker delegates for the City Hall program while preserving anonymity and voluntary participation.

correction: Continuity work and overlapping infrastructure do not identify an R sender or prove autonomy, intent, or harm to Vale. The incomplete article rumor is unnecessary to establish worker harm, and the route evidence does not justify declaring Vale dead.
goal_progress: Preserved the protected worker’s anonymity while allowing the systemic labor fact to circulate.
Confirmed C03’s testimony boundary covering names, routes, identifying details, and consent.
Converted C05’s institutional remedy into a concrete demand for worker-elected governance and independent enforcement.
Accepted C11’s distinct political remedy and demanded written representation, anonymous reporting, nonretaliation, and worker voting seats.
Separated worker harm from the unresolved article rumor and from theories about R or Vale.
reasoning_summary: The strongest reconstruction keeps three questions separate. The record supports collaborative human labor and continuity capability, but neither proves a single human author nor autonomous synthetic control. Vale’s limited access makes investigation plausible, while the manufactured route segment, unidentified service exit, and missing verified trail leave fate unresolved. Worker protection therefore cannot depend on solving the mystery.
